EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding caused by rapid snowmelt in March continued through mid-April. The Silvies river rose to 14.76 feet, about half a foot under the record of 15.2 feet, on March 29th. The water levels began to decrease, although the area remained inundated. Another warm period in mid-April allowed for the river level to begin rising again. Reaching a secondary peak of 12.58 feet on April 11th. Water dropped below flood stage on April 15th. Flood impacts were observed through mid-late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of homes were impacted by flood waters, many with major damage and some were uninhabitable. Numerous residents were evacuated during the height of the flooding with some sheltering in hotels and Red Cross mass care facilities. Extensive damage to sewer system.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front passage enhanced by an atmospheric river produced bands of moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of our mountain zones within the atmospheric river path through the weekend. Ahead of the trough passage, an area of enhanced lift developed over central Oregon, resulting in heavy snowfall occurring the afternoon the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hog Pass SNOTEL measured 16 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1700 PDT on March 15th. A social media post 11 miles south southwest of Three Rivers, OR reported 23 inches of new storm total snow at 2000 on March 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and surface cold front passage enhanced by an atmospheric river produced bands of moderate to heavy snowfall across portions of our mountain zones within the atmospheric river path through the weekend. Ahead of the trough passage, an area of enhanced lift developed over central Oregon, resulting in heavy snowfall occurring the afternoon the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs station OR-DS-17, that is 2.3 miles west northwest of Bend, reported a storm total snow amount of 6 inches at 0700 PDT on March 17th, with estimated end of snowfall between 1815 PDT to 1900 PDT on March 16th. The Cocorahs station OR-DS-44, that is 3.5 miles east southeast of Bend, reported a storm total snow amount of 4.3 inches at 0700 PDT on March 17th, with estimated end of snowfall between 1815 PDT to 1900 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rowena Fire started the afternoon of the 11th near the community of Rowena, and quickly spread up hill and east towards The Dalles as winds increased to around 30 mph that afternoon. Dry, warm, and breezy conditions assisted the spread of the wildfire after the 11th. Fuel conditions were also dry enough for the wildfire to spread quickly when it first started.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wildfire resulted in numerous evacuations in and around Rowena and Chenowith, Oregon. As of the most recent official count performed June 15th, a total of 56 residential structures and 91 additional structures, including sheds, barns and shops, were destroyed by the fire. The fire also resulted in all lanes of Interstate 84 between mile posts 64 and 82 to be closed between 1330 PDT on the 11th to 0520 PDT on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A break from the long series of frontal systems occurred from 12/30/2024 into 1/1/2025. This allowed the Coquille River at Coquille to drop below flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille began the month in minor flood stage at 22.45 ft. The river steadily dropped during 01/01/2025, and fell below minor flood stage of 21.0 ft. at 01/1430 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A new series of frontal systems began on 01/01/2025 and caused the Coquille River at Coquille to rise above minor flood stage again from 01/04 into 01/06, although with a lower peak than occurred at the end of December 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above minor flood stage of 21.0 ft. at 04/145 PST, peaked in minor flood stage at 05/945 PST at 22.50 ft. It then fell below flood stage at 06/1745 PST.

CZ_NAME: FOOTHILLS OF THE NORTHERN BLUE MOUNTAINS OF OREGON
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pendleton WFO measured 4.2 inches of new snow in a 36 hour period ending 0400 on February 5th. The superintendent reported 4 inches of new snow at 1000 Feb. 5th. Radar and observation estimated end of snow occurred before 0400 on February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs station OR-WL-1 in Joseph reported 13.3 inches of new snow in an 18 hour period ending at 0200 Feb. 5th. A FB post received at 1200 on Feb. 5th reported 12 inches of new snow had fallen 2 miles west of Enterprise. Radar and observation estimated end of snowfall was before 0500 on February 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cocorahs observer OR-DS-44 3 miles east southeast of Bend reported 4.4 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0700 February 4th. The Cocorahs observer OR-CK-13 9 miles southeast of Redmond reported 3.5 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 0700 February 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system, a weak to moderate atmospheric river, and the development of a stationary front across the area resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snow across the mountains and lower elevations of eastern Oregon and southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ochoco Meadows Snotel measured 6 inches of new snow in a 34 hour period ending at 0100 on Feb. 5th. The Cocorahs observer OR-GR-4 9 miles northwest of Seneca measured 6.9 inches of new snow in a 48 hour period ending at 0700 on Feb. 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold conditions were in place early on February 13th as widespread precipitation increased across the region south to north due to the arrival of a warm-frontal boundary. This overrunning scenario led to a mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain impacting much of western Oregon through February 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary on the northeastern periphery of an undercutting shortwave trough combined with locally cooler temperatures and a period of offshore flow to produce locally lower snow-levels and warning level snow amounts across the Hood River Valley the night of January 1st into January 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 0700 on 01/02, a trained spotter reported 6in of new snowfall just outside of Parkdale, OR.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low and accompanying frontal boundary dug southward into the region from off the coast of British Columbia bringing heavy snowfall to portions of the Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hogg Pass SNOTEL at 4800 ft recorded 12 inches of snowfall between 0300 1/31 and 1300 01/31 with 12 inches hit 1300 01/31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely strong west-southwesterly winds aloft carried tropical moisture into the area. In southeast Oregon, winds mixed down to the surface causing significant wind speeds to be observed at all elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wagontire RAWS recorded a peak gust of 64 knots (74 mph) in Harney county, Oregon due to high winds that mixed to the surface from an atmospheric river. Sustained winds at Wagontire were 39 knots (45 mph) during the same hour that peak gusts were observed. At the same time, Riddle Mountain mesonet had a peak gust of 60 knots (70 mph), Basque Hills mesonet reached 57 knots (66 mph), and Frenchglen mesonet reached 55 knots (63 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely strong west-southwesterly winds aloft carried tropical moisture into the area. In southeast Oregon, winds mixed down to the surface causing significant wind speeds to be observed at all elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Burns Junction mesonet recorded a peak gust of 62 knots (71 mph) in Malheur county, Oregon due to high winds that mixed to the surface from an atmospheric river. Sustained winds at Burns Junction were 37 knots (42 mph) during the same hour that peak gusts were observed. At the same time, Grassy Mountain mesonet had a peak gust of 50 knots (58 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low and accompanying frontal boundary dug southward into the region from off the coast of British Columbia bringing heavy snowfall to portions of the Oregon Cascades. The post frontal showers were also strong enough to produce a funnel cloud in Linn County on Feb 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hogg Pass SNOTEL at 4800 ft recorded 12 inches of snowfall between 0300 1/31 and 1300 01/31 with 12 inches hit 1300 01/31, and the Bear Grass SNOTEL at 4720 ft recorded 14 inches of snowfall between 0200 1/31 and 1800 02/01 with 12 inches hit 0100 02/01.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska brought heavy snow to elevations above 5000 feet in the southern Oregon Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crater Lake National Park reported 12 inches of snow on 1/31, while the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated partial storm totals of 6 to 15 inches by the end of the day. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.55 inches at Clearwater, 1.05 inches at Mt. Stella, and 1.28 inches at Elbow Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Multnomah County Medical Examiner reported that a man died from hyperthermia on Feb 2, 2025 in the 97219 ZIP code.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Multnomah County Medical Examiner reported that a man died from hyperthermia on Feb 2 in the 97219 ZIP code. The morning temperatures were around 35 degrees Fahrenheit with light precipitation and calm winds. It is unclear whether the death was due to the temperatures from this day or a prolonged exposure to cold as the overnight temperatures were around 25 degrees from January 25 through 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Surf closed the Columbia River Bar impacting marine commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The offshore marine buoy 46029 reported 23 to 34 ft swells from the afternoon on Feb 24 through the afternoon on Feb 25. The high surf resulted in the United States Coast Guard closing the Columbia River Bar to marine traffic for almost 24 hours. This is a financial loss as 63 million dollars worth of commerce moves across the Columbia River Bar per day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening upper-level low and an associated strong low pressure system at the surface tracked just off the Pacific Northwest coast leading to a period of high winds. Warmer temperatures, additional rainfall and prior snowfall to this event facilitated localized flooding as well. The frontal passage during the early afternoon hours of Feb 24th allowed for a period of isolated severe thunderstorms later that afternoon due to a low CAPE but high shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yaquina Bay Bridge measured a peak gust of 74 mph 1544 02/24 PST, and was near or above 58 mph 1430 02/24 to 0300 02/25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt due to warm temperatures caused the Silvies River to rise to 14.76 feet, about half a foot under the record of 15.2 feet. This caused significant flooding in and around Burns, Oregon while the river was in flood stage from March 25th through April 3rd and from April 10th through April 15th. Flooding was visible over the area between the two flood stage events and for a few days after April 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of homes were impacted by flood waters, many with major damage and some were uninhabitable. Numerous residents were evacuated during the height of the flooding with some sheltering in hotels and Red Cross mass care facilities. There was extensive damage to sewer system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough held over the far eastern Pacific leading to near sea-level snowfall. Heavier shower bands facilitated pockets of warning-level snowfall through this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local Storm Report: Public measured 3 inches of snow at 100ft in Tillamook on 2/4 858 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level trough passages resulted in rounds of showers and thunderstorms developing across the region the afternoon of May 17th. As one of these storms developed near the town of Hermiston, a funnel cloud was able to develop at the base of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public provided a photo of a funnel cloud that occurred near the the town of Hermiston. Social media posts from local newspapers also provided other images of the funnel cloud taken around the Hermiston area. Location is estimated from radar and orientation of discernable landmarks in the photos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A board low pressure system facilitated unsettled conditions across the region with cold air aloft supporting weak afternoon instability. During the afternoon hours of May 12th a cold air funnel cloud was reported in shower activity near Eugene, Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photographs and video from social media, in addition to a special METAR from KEUG, indicate a funnel cloud developed around 1-2 miles northwest of the Eugene Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warming temperatures and rain on snow events led to quick rises in the Malheur River. This led to several levee breaks near Vale, OR and caused agricultural flooding and nuisance flooding over Highway 20.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Personnel from Boise WFO surveyed flooding on the Malheur River on Wednesday, February 26th. The river reached flood stage (19.5 feet) at Vale (VALO3) on Monday, February 24th around 9 pm and continued to rise into the major flood category reaching a crest of 23.55 feet around 12:00 pm MST on February 25th. The crest of 23.55 feet represents a stage above the USGS rating curve but estimated to be around 11,300 cfs, which is the highest flow since 1982. Observed impacts found by the NWS flood survey, conducted on February 26th, included some houses and outbuildings being impacted by flood water, especially in the vicinity of the 36th street bridge, in Ontario, Oregon. Significant flooding of agricultural land was observed near the river, extending as far as 1/2 a mile from the main channel on both sides of the river. There were several irrigation canals where water from the river breached head gates over filling the canals with flood water. At least one breach of the Nevada Irrigation canal side levy was observed which was causing additional agricultural flooding in an area separated from the main river flooding near Vale Oregon. U.S Highway 20, west of Vale, Oregon was inundated with flood water in several sections and remains closed as of 10 am, February 26th. It appears that the main levy protecting the city of Vale, remained intact through the flood. Information from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, indicated that any rise of water above 24 feet would have exceeded the safe design of the levee, and the observed crest of 23.55 feet remained below that critical threshold. There have not been any reported fatalities or injuries. The river has receded from the flood crest and fell below flood stage around 10 am, February 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through eastern Oregon, winds increased behind the frontal passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station OD143, 3.6 SE Huntington, recorded a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On 06/30/2025, an area of low pressure along the northern California coast produced numerous thunderstorms in south central Oregon and northern California. Downdraft winds associated with one thunderstorm 5 miles north of Keno in Klamath County broke off several 1 inch diameter limbs from a healthy tree. Also, 1.63 inches of rain were measured from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level low pressure disturbance over Vancouver Island and an antecedent frontal boundary facilitated a period of showery post-frontal convection on the afternoon of August 6th. This activity was robust enough produce an isolated and short lived Landspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout was photographed and briefly recorded to the west-northwest of Central Linn High School in the fields around Halsey, OR. No damage was reported. A small condensation funnel was observed above the ground circulation by the observer even though the photographs and video did not show the condensation funnel.  The ground circulation and condensation funnel were not visibly connected. Time is based on the public report. Location and path width are roughly estimated based on video footage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust area of high pressure over the eastern Pacific shifted towards Oregon August 10th, remaining overhead on the 11th before finally retrograding further west late on August 12th. High temperatures peaked in the 90s to near 100 each day during this period across the inland valleys combined with overnight lows in the 60s leading to exacerbated heat related impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two fatalities presumed heat related of a Male, 59 in the 97233 zip code and a Female, 58 in 97214 zip code. Emergency managers and health officials reported elevated Multnomah County 911 calls due to the heat and higher than normal hospital/urgent care heat related visits.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure centered over the Four Corners region shifted northward into the Pacific Northwest leading to well above normal temperatures through this period. High temperatures peaked in the 90s to 100-105 range across the inland valleys with overnight low temperatures in the 60s to low 70s exacerbating heat related impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a couple of fatalities presumed to be due to the Heat with a Male, 56, in 97209 zip code, and a Female, 55 (Clackamas County). Additionally there were reported elevated County 911 calls due to the heat and elevated hospital/urgent care heat related visits for both Multnomah county and Clackamas county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cape Blanco buoy (46015) and the Umpqua Offshore buoy (46229) reported swell sufficient to generate high surf. Surf heights reached 27 to 32 feet. Waves were highest on west facing beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska continued to bring heavy snow to elevations above 5000 feet in the southern Oregon Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of April 17th, northerly flow over the Pacific Northwest allowed for temperatures to drop below freezing for much of the lowlands of the forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various co-op and mesonet stations in the area, including E1881 and D9612, as well as CPC sites, all recorded sub-freezing temperatures the morning of the 17th, primarily during a 2-hour period between 5:30am and 7:30am PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure centered offshore of southern Oregon and far northern California sent mid-level moisture northward and created an unstable air mass, leading to the development of a severe thunderstorm with strong straight line, downdraft winds in the Rogue Valley cities of Central Point, Medford, and Jacksonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds downed 13 110 ft. tall wooden power poles along Highway 238 between Central Point and Jacksonville. A car was totaled after it drove into one of the poles. There were many fallen trees and limbs, which damaged 3 vehicles in Central Point.  Power lines also were downed along Table Rock Rd. in Medford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 26th, Southwest flow aloft worked to advect midlevel moisture across eastern Oregon. Throughout the afternoon, surface heating destabilized the airmass and resulted in scattered thunderstorm development. While initial development was over higher terrain, increasing shear values allowed for storms to sustain themselves while moving off of the terrain. With a dry boundary layer, severe winds were the primary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a thunderstorm passed over head, the Ontario airport ASOS recorded two severe wind gusts. The first one had a magnitude of 50kt occurring at 1848MST. Later, at 1856MST, the ASOS measured a gust of 58kt from the same thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river and stationary front brought heavy snow and gusty winds with the most significant impacts on 02/03 (Monday morning). The snowfall accumulation was high across the area, which was unusually high for areas east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 4 to 10 inches.  Precipitation amounts in the zone included 1.22 inches at Buckeye, and 1.25 inches at Toketee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 29th, southwest flow aloft worked to advect mid-level moisture across eastern Oregon. During the afternoon, an embedded shortwave in the flow allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop. The afternoon Boise sounding showed a dry boundary layer, allowing for the possibility of severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Foster Flat RAWS measured a wind gust of 54 knots, originating from an outflow of a nearby thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 31st, a shortwave trough embedded in southwesterly flow aloft moved over eastern Oregon. This forcing, paired with increasing moisture in the southwesterly flow, allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baker County Emergency Management reported debris over the road within the Durkee burn area. Timing was estimated using Hydrophobic Unit Streamflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river and stationary front brought heavy snow and gusty winds with the most significant impacts on 02/03 (Monday morning). The snowfall accumulation was high across the area, which was unusually high for areas east of the Cascades.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 12 to 30 inches, including 12 to 18 inches at valley locations. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 2.46 inches at Klamath Falls International Airport, 2.93 inches at Worden, and 3.53 inches at Lorella.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong gradient between antecedent cold, high pressure and a cold front moving southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska produced high winds in southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak south winds recorded along the Curry County coast included 42G51 MPH at Port Orford, 41G59 MPH at Gold Beach Airport, and 29G67 MPH at Flynn Prairie RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong occluding front moving southeast out of the Gulf of Alaska brought high south winds to the Curry County coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds reached a peak of 47G56 MPH at Port Orford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strongest of a series of frontal systems stalled over southern Oregon on 03/16. It produced heavy rain that caused numerous rivers in southwest Oregon to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cow Creek near Riddle rose above minor flood stage of 22.0 ft. at 16/955 PST, peaked in moderate flood stage at 16/1545 PST at 25.64 ft. It then fell below flood stage at 16/2227 PST. At 22.0 feet, flooding of low-lying fields and structures begins at this point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 19th, a shortwave embedded in southwesterly flow crossed over eastern Oregon and southwest Idaho. Sufficient mid-level moisture paired with the synoptic lift allowed thunderstorms to develop. With a dry boundary layer being observed, wind gusts were the primary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station BQFO3 Basque Hills recorded a wind gust of 59MPH associated with a passing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry atmospheric conditions and with sufficiently dried grass allowed a brush fire to quickly spread during the afternoon, resulting in area evacuations and destruction of property.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire started north of Hermiston around 1500 PDT and resulted in evacuations within the area. The fire also spread to a grass lot, with media photos showing several school buses destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moved into southern Oregon and northern California on 3/15 then became nearly stationary, oriented from northeast to southwest across the area, from 3/16 into 3/17. Additional snow showers behind the main system lingered into 3/18. The heaviest snow occurred during 3/16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 18 to 54 inches at elevations above 4500 feet. A snowfall total of 48.7 inches was measured at Crater Lake. Precipitation amounts measured included: 4.40 inches at Diamond Lake, 3.40 inches at Summit Lake, 4.10 inches at New Crescent Lake, and 2.70 inches at Annie Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 3rd and 4th, southwest flow aloft was dominant across eastern Oregon. This flow aloft advected mid-level in moisture across eastern Oregon. A shortwave and wind speed maximum ejecting through the southwesterly flow increased the available shear and instability for thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grassy Mountain RAWS site recorded a wind gust of 58 mph. No damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station OD152 recorded a 58 mph wind gust as showers passed overhead. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 9th, an upper level low tracked northeast across eastern Oregon aiding in thunderstorm development. With a dry boundary layer, and marginal precipitable water values, outflow winds were the primary hazard with burn scar flooding a secondary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm passed over the Cow Valley burn scar. Radar estimated hourly QPE ranging from 1.5 to 1.75 inches with the three hourly estimate having a peak of 2.0 to 2.5 inches. The Oregon Department of Transportation reported a debris flow across US 26 between mile markers 249-251, resulting in traffic delays. Timing was estimated using hydrophobic unit streamflow, which peaked at 6-10 cubic meters per second per kilometer squared.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple weather spotters and members of the public posting on Facebook reported severe hail of 1 to 1.5 inches falling around southern and eastern Bend, Oregon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: ODOT sensors recorded thunderstorm gusts up to 63 mph associated with a severe cell that tracked north northwest over Bend, Oregon. This storm also produced severe hail around the Bend area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 3rd, an incoming upper level trough was positioned west of eastern Oregon and an associated cold front moved across eastern Oregon. Thunderstorms developed ahead of and along the cold front as it moved west to east. Model soundings indicated a dry boundary layer (inverted-V soundings) in eastern Oregon during convective initiation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kelsey Butte RAWS measured a wind gust of 54 knots associated with a passing thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low off the west coast sent two cold fronts across southern Oregon with gusty southerly winds. Snow levels were high, at around 5500 feet, with the first front from 12/24 . It brought moderate snow. Heavy snow then occurred at the valley floors of south central Oregon with the slower, colder, stronger second front on 12/25 and post-frontal snow showers on 12/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 6 to 18 inches. Amounts were highest at elevations above 5000 feet. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.63 inches at Howard Prairie Dam, and 0.80 inches at Cold Springs Camp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A person was knocked off of their feet by a sneaker wave while walking on the beach near the mouth of Siletz Bay. They were then swept out to sea by a rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was walking on the sand near the mouth of Siletz Bay when a sneaker wave knocked them off of their feet and dragged them into the ocean.  Once in the water, a strong rip current quickly swept them out to sea. They were swept out far enough that the people on the beach could not see them. The person did not survive, and their body was found on the beach around 4 miles south from where they were swept in. The 3 pm buoy observation from buoy 46050 reported a west swell of 11ft with a 16 second period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low quickly deepened offshore due west of the mouth of the Columbia River and progressed towards the Olympic Peninsula allowing for a tight surface pressure gradient to drive strong to locally high winds region-wide. This surface gradient was also aided by a strong band of west-southwest winds just above the surface (often called a sting jet) which pivoted over the region through the day on Oct. 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1743 PST a peak gust of 60mph was measured by a mesonet station at the Astoria-Megler Bridge. This mesonet station was at or near warning criteria from 1300 to 1930 PST on October 25th.

Additionally, a 59 mph peak wind gusts was measured at the at Pacific City Mesonet station AT297 at 1428, and AT297 was at or near warning criteria 1200 to 1600 PST.

Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.73 inches at Round Lake Ridge, 0.62 inches at Klamath Falls International Airport, and 0.76 inches at Lost River at Bonanza.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN & EASTERN KLAMATH COUNTY & WESTERN LAKE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level low off the west coast sent two cold fronts across southern Oregon with gusty southerly winds. Snow levels were high, at around 5500 feet, with the first front from 12/24 . It brought moderate snow. Heavy snow then occurred at the valley floors of south central Oregon with the slower, colder, stronger second front on 12/25 and post-frontal snow showers on 12/26.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Gridded Snowfall Analysis indicated storm totals of 3 to 12 inches. Precipitation amounts in the zone included 0.80 inches at Crazyman Flat, 0.82 inches at Strawberry, and 0.80 inches at Silver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-December's synoptic regime was dominated by a potent atmospheric river that caused widespread flooding on the west coast and persistent precipitation in the intermountain west. Forcing was largely provided by a stationary front along the river, with shortwaves in the mid-level flow allowing it to change from warm to cold front every few days as it kept active weather in the area. However, a deep mid-level trough would strengthen on the West Coast, and as it made landfall December 17th and 18th it pushed a surface low of 980 Mean Sea Level Pressure with winds at 700 millibars of near 100 knots. The strong cold front associated with the surface low, strong winds in the low-levels from the Pressure Gradient Force of the low and the atmospheric river, and the persistent moisture supply from the atmospheric river was the perfect environment for severe wind outbreak across southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho.||Unlike typical stratiform winter passages, this event featured embedded convective showers and thunderstorms. This convective element enhanced the transport of high-momentum air to the surface. It was noted that the atmospheric remained fully coupled throughout the event, a very rare occurrence in mid-December. The frontal passage brought winds of near 60 miles per hour across the area, while the morning squall along the front saw winds up to 80 miles per hour, with one ridgeline observation of over 100 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather observations across Baker County indicated 60-65 mile per hour winds across much of the I-84 transportation corridor. As well as winds up to 85 miles per hour near the Snake River and in highlands as measured by the Halfway mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system supported by a negatively tilted upper-level trough drove a cold-frontal boundary through the region supporting a period of high winds. Directly behind this frontal boundary a weakly unstable but highly sheared atmospheric environment lead to thunderstorms, one of which was severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 66 mph peak wind gust was measured at Mesonet station ODT76, Astoria-Megler Bridge with gusts above or near warning criteria 1130 Nov 5th to 1500 Nov 6th. The ASOS KAST also measured a peak gust of 60 mph at 1345 Nov 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorm activity during the late afternoon and evening hours produced a local report of a funnel cloud in central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chief Meteorologist from KTVZ in Bend, OR shared a viewer video of a funnel cloud briefly touching down near Crooked River Ranch Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 22nd, Scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern Oregon as an upper level trough moved across the area. Precipitable water values were near the 50th percentile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baker County emergency management conducted a ground survey the day following the storm. Evidence was found that water had flowed across Rye Valley Lane and Mormon Basin Road. In addition, rocks, mud and debris were deposited on these roads in at least two locations, indicating that debris flow activity also occurred. Nearby residences reported heavy rainfall, consistent with radar estimates of 1.13 inches of rain falling in less than 30 minutes. Exact location and timing were estimated using hydrophobic unit flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a cold front brought strong south winds to the Curry County Coast in southern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were recorded at Flynn Prairie with south winds reaching a peak of 26 mph with gusts to 59 mph, and at Gold Beach Municipal Airport with a peak of 44 mph with gusts to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies behind a cold front boundary resulted in temperatures cooling to below freezing for several hours across much of north central Oregon the morning of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various Oregon Dept of Transportation and mesonet stations across North Central Oregon recorded sub-freezing temperatures for several hours between 0200 PST and 0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and high pressure overhead allowed for radiative cooling processes to take over and cool the eastern Columbia River Gorge to below freezing across much of the zone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various mesonet and Oregon Department of Transportation stations as well as the ASOS at the Dalles Municipal Airport reported sub-freezing temperatures for several hours between 2am and 8am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Summer began winding down, a strong trough pushed a cold front through southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho. The timing coincided with peak instability, allowing thunderstorms to form as the front passed. These storms exhibited locally heavy rainfall, but even more significant local wind gusts observed in southeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed near Burns Junction shortly after the Rome storm, and produced even stronger wind. The mesonet near Burns Junction recorded gusts over 60 mph for about 10 minutes, with a peak gust of 71 mph. Sustained winds were still near 40 mph, speaking to the quick moving nature of the winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rome airport observed gusts near 60 mph for about 5 minutes as a thunderstorms gust front move through. Sustained winds were between 36-44 mph at the same time. This occurred shortly before an even stronger gust occurred at Burns Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Emigrant Wildfire was discovered on August 24th. The fire started 24 miles southeast of Oakridge in Lane County, Oregon and burned into Douglas County, Oregon. The fire was caused by lightning. As of 530 AM PST on September 30th, it had burned 33,096 acres, was 49% contained, no structures had been destroyed, and it had cost $ 56.9M.
